---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: true
    pdf-engine: xelatex
incremental: false
header-includes: |
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  \usepackage{tikz}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric, fit, backgrounds, calc}
  \usepackage{booktabs}
  \usepackage{xcolor}
  \usepackage{array}
  \definecolor{mblue}{RGB}{35,55,100}
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="beamer"}
```{=latex}
\title{Clinical Research Methodology\\
\vspace{0.5em}
{\normalsize \textbf{13. Reviews and Meta-analysis}}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}
\titlegraphic{
\vspace*{7cm}
}
\maketitle
```
:::

# 12.1 Evidence Synthesis

## Introduction

```{=latex}
% [SUGGESTED IMAGE: classic 6-level EBM pyramid with gradient colors]
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.85, font=\tiny]
  \foreach \i/\lbl/\shade in {
    0/{Expert Opinions}/22,
    1/{Case Series \& Case Reports}/30,
    2/{Case-Control Studies}/40,
    3/{Cohort Studies}/52,
    4/{RCT}/70,
    5/{Meta-analysis \& \\ Systematic Review}/90
  }{
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\w}{6.5 - \i*0.75}
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\y}{\i*0.6}
    \fill[mblue!\shade] (-\w/2, \y) rectangle (\w/2, \y+0.55);
    \node[white, font=\tiny\bfseries, align=center] at (0, \y+0.27) {\lbl};
  }
  \node[right, font=\scriptsize, mblue] at (3.5, 3.3) {Strength};
  \node[right, font=\scriptsize, mblue] at (3.5, 3.0) {of evidence $\uparrow$};
  \draw[-{Stealth}, mblue, thick] (3.5, 0.3) -- (3.5, 2.9);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{-0.3em}
\small The \textbf{Evidence Based Medicine} (EBM) pyramid organizes study designs by \textbf{strength of the evidence produced}.
```

## Why synthesize evidence?

\small

No single study is sufficient to ground clinical practice:

```{=latex}
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item Samples are limited $\Rightarrow$ unstable estimates \pause
\item Results can contradict each other \pause
\item It is very time-consuming to read all original articles \pause
\item Guidelines must be based on a body of evidence, not on single works.
\end{itemize}
```

## Evidence Synthesis

By **Evidence Synthesis** we mean the process of collecting, organizing and comprehensively evaluating existing results on a specific topic.

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.7,
                    box/.style={draw,rounded corners,fill=mblue!20,text width=2cm,
                    align=center,font=\footnotesize,minimum height=0.7cm},
                    arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]
\node[box] (s1) at (-3.5,0) {Study 1};
\node[box] (s2) at ( 0,0) {Study 2};
\node[box] (s3) at ( 3.5,0) {Study 3};
\node[box] (sr) at ( 0,-2.5) {\textbf{Synthesis}\\\tiny (Review / Meta-analysis)};
\node[box] (lg) at ( 0,-5.0) {\textbf{Guidelines}\\\tiny (Clinical practice)};
\draw[arr] (s1)--(sr); \draw[arr] (s2)--(sr); \draw[arr] (s3)--(sr);
\draw[arr] (sr)--(lg);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

## Types of Reviews: Overview

```{=latex}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  box/.style={draw,rounded corners,fill=mblue!15,text width=3.5cm,
  align=center,font=\footnotesize,minimum height=1.2cm},
]
\node[box] at (-5,1.2) {\textbf{Narrative}\\[3pt]\tiny Flexible, critical, not systematic. Risk of selection bias.};
\node[box] at (-1,1.2)    {\textbf{Scoping}\\[3pt]\tiny Maps the breadth of a field. No quantitative synthesis.};
\node[box] at (3,1.2)  {\textbf{Systematic}\\[3pt]\tiny Predefined protocols, exhaustive search, risk-of-bias assessment.};
\node[box] at (-5,-0.5){\textbf{Umbrella Review}\\[3pt]\tiny Synthesis of already existing systematic reviews.};
\node[box] at (-1,-0.5)   {\textbf{Living Review}\\[3pt]\tiny Continuous updating with new studies.};
\node[box] at (3,-0.5) {\textbf{Meta-analysis}\\[3pt]\tiny \emph{Quantitative} synthesis of effect sizes from multiple studies.};
\end{tikzpicture}
```

## Systematic Review

\small

**Objective**: synthesize the evidence on a specific aspect of scientific interest.

**Characteristics**:

-   Predefined and registered protocol (e.g. PROSPERO) \emph{before} starting the search.

-   Exhaustive search: multiple databases (PubMed, Embase, PsycINFO, Cochrane), grey literature, reference lists of included studies.

-   Double selection: two independent reviewers select studies and resolve disagreements by consensus or a third reviewer.

-   Risk-of-bias assessment: for each included study (e.g. RoB 2 for RCTs, ROBINS-I for observational studies).

-   Output can be narrative or, when possible, a quantitative meta-analysis.

\tiny PROSPERO: \url{https://www.crd.york.ac.uk/prospero/}\\ RoB 2: \href{https://sites.google.com/site/riskofbiastool/welcome/rob-2-0-tool?authuser=0}{\underline{ROB2 link}}

## Systematic Review: advantages and disadvantages

**advantages**:

-   less prone to bias
-   replicable

**disadvantages**:

-   more complex to conduct
-   can give the illusion of complete mapping: the initial query can influence the results.

# 12.2 Meta-Analysis

## What is a Meta-Analysis?

\small

A meta-analysis combines **quantitatively** the results of multiple studies to obtain a pooled estimate of the effect size, with greater precision than the individual studies.

\vspace{0.4em}

**Basic rationale**: summing the information increases statistical power and the stability of the estimates.

\vspace{0.4em}

**Fundamental ingredients**:

\begin{enumerate}\footnotesize
\item Study selection (from a systematic review)
\item Extraction of effect sizes (Cohen's $d$, OR, RR, correlation $r$, \ldots)
\item Assessment of \textbf{risk of bias} for each study
\item Model choice: \emph{fixed effect} vs \emph{random effects}
\item Visualization: \textbf{Forest Plot} and \textbf{Funnel Plot}
\item Heterogeneity tests ($I^2$, Cochran's $Q$)
\end{enumerate}

## Fixed Effect vs Random Effects

\begin{columns}
\column{0.5\textwidth}
\textbf{Fixed Effect}
\vspace{0.3em}

\footnotesize Assumes a single underlying true effect. All studies estimate the same parameter (variation is only sampling variation).

\vspace{0.3em}
Rarely justified in psychology: different studies, different populations, different measures.

\column{0.5\textwidth}
\textbf{Random Effects}
\vspace{0.3em}

\footnotesize Assumes that each study estimates an effect drawn from a distribution of true effects. Heterogeneity is part of the model.

\vspace{0.3em}
More realistic in psychology/neuropsychology. Produces wider CIs (more honest).
\end{columns}

\vspace{0.5em}

\small \textbf{Rule of thumb}: in the presence of substantial heterogeneity ($I^2 > 50\%$), the random effects model is preferable. However, high heterogeneity may indicate that a meta-analysis is not appropriate.

## Forest Plot

```{=latex}
% [SUGGESTED IMAGE: classic forest plot from a neuropsychological meta-analysis article]
% Alternatively, the following R code generates a forest plot with metafor:
% library(metafor)
% dat <- escalc(measure="SMD",
%   m1i=c(0.5,0.3,0.8,0.4,0.6),
%   sd1i=c(1,1,1,1,1), n1i=c(30,25,40,20,35),
%   m2i=c(0,0,0,0,0),
%   sd2i=c(1,1,1,1,1), n2i=c(30,25,40,20,35))
% dat$study <- c("Study A","Study B","Study C","Study D","Study E")
% res <- rma(yi,vi,data=dat)
% forest(res, slab=dat$study, xlab="Cohen's d", header="Study")
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.82, font=\tiny]
  % Axes
  \draw[thick,->] (-3,0) -- (3,0) node[right]{Cohen's $d$};
  \draw[dashed,gray] (0,-0.3)--(0,5.5);
  \node[below,font=\scriptsize,gray] at (0,-0.35) {0};

  % Studies (point estimate + CI)
  \foreach \y/\est/\lo/\hi/\lbl/\w in {
    4.8 / 0.6 / 0.1 / 1.1 / {Study A (n=60)}  / 3pt,
    3.9 / 0.3 /-0.2 / 0.8 / {Study B (n=50)}  / 2.5pt,
    3.0 / 0.9 / 0.5 / 1.3 / {Study C (n=80)}  / 4pt,
    2.1 / 0.4 /-0.1 / 0.9 / {Study D (n=40)}  / 2pt,
    1.2 / 0.7 / 0.2 / 1.2 / {Study E (n=70)}  / 3.5pt
  }{
    \draw[mblue!60] (\lo,\y)--(\hi,\y);
    \fill[mblue!80] (\est,\y) circle (\w);
    \node[left,font=\tiny] at (-3.1,\y) {\lbl};
  }
  % Pooled (diamond)
  \fill[mblue] (-0.05,0.2) -- (0.6,0.5) -- (1.25,0.2) -- (0.6,-0.1) -- cycle;
  \node[left,font=\scriptsize\bfseries] at (-3.1,0.2) {Pooled ($d = 0.60$)};
  \node[above,font=\tiny,mblue] at (0.6,0.5) {$[0.38,\; 0.82]$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{-0.3em}
\small The \textbf{Forest Plot} shows the effect size and its 95\% CI for each study. The \textbf{diamond} represents the pooled estimate. Larger circles = studies with greater weight (larger n).
```

## Funnel Plot and Publication Bias

![](../Figures/Funnel_plot.png){fig-align="center" width="80%"}

```{=latex}


\small A \textbf{symmetric funnel plot} indicates absence of publication bias. \textbf{Asymmetry} (an empty left side) suggests that small studies with negative effects have not been published.
```

## Heterogeneity: $I^2$ and Cochran's $Q$

\small

**Cochran's** $Q$: tests whether the variability between studies is greater than that expected by chance alone. Low power with few studies.

\vspace{0.3em}

$I^2$ (Higgins): percentage of total variability attributable to true (non-sampling) heterogeneity.

\vspace{0.3em}

| $I^2$                 | Conventional interpretation              |
|-----------------------|------------------------------------------|
| \footnotesize 0--25%  | \footnotesize Low heterogeneity          |
| \footnotesize 25--50% | \footnotesize Moderate heterogeneity     |
| \footnotesize 50--75% | \footnotesize Substantial heterogeneity  |
| \footnotesize $>75\%$ | \footnotesize Considerable heterogeneity |

\vspace{0.3em}

\small \textbf{Caution}: high heterogeneity does not mean that the meta-analysis is wrong, but that the pooled estimate must be interpreted with care. A **subgroup** analysis or a **meta-regression** may be necessary.

## Heterogeneity: further reading

For a deeper look, see \href{https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12326561/}{\underline{\textbf{Choi et al., 2025}}}

## Assessing Publication Bias

\small

**Funnel plot** (visual): subjective; reliable only with $\geq 10$ studies.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Egger's test**: regression of the effect size on its precision: an intercept $\neq 0$ suggests asymmetry.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Trim-and-Fill** (Duval & Tweedie): estimates how many "missing" studies there would be in a symmetric scenario and corrects the pooled estimate.

\vspace{0.3em}

$p$-curve and $z$-curve: analyze the distribution of $p$-values: they can detect $p$-hacking and selective reporting even without a funnel plot.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Fail-safe N** (Rosenthal): how many negative studies would be needed to nullify the result? Less used today.

\vspace{0.2em}

\tiny Carter et al. (2019). Correcting for bias in psychology: A comparison of meta-analytic methods. \emph{Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science}. Open Access: \url{https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/2515245919847196}

## Risk of Bias (RoB)

\small

Each study included in the meta-analysis is assessed for its **risk of bias**, which influences the weight it is given and the confidence in the conclusions.

\vspace{0.4em}

**Main tools**:

\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item \textbf{RoB 2} (Cochrane, for RCTs): 5 domains — randomization, deviations from intervention, missing data, outcome measurement, selection of reported results
\item \textbf{ROBINS-I} (for observational studies): 7 domains
\item \textbf{QUADAS-2} (for diagnostic studies): 4 domains
\item \textbf{Newcastle-Ottawa Scale}: for cohort and case-control studies
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.4em}

**How RoB is used in a meta-analysis**: sensitivity analysis excluding high-risk studies; "GRADE" for the overall level of evidence.

\vspace{0.2em}

\tiny Sterne et al. (2019). RoB 2: a revised tool for RCTs. \emph{BMJ} 366:l4898. Open Access: \url{https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.l4898}

## Main Limitations of Meta-analyses

```{=latex}


\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners, minimum width=3cm, minimum height=1cm, align=center, fill=mblue!20,}
]
  \node[box] (Gin) {Garbage in};
  \node[box, right=2cm of Gin] (Gout) {Garbage out};
  \draw[->, thick] (Gin) -- (Gout);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

## Limitations of Meta-analyses

\small

**"Garbage in, garbage out"**: if the primary studies have serious methodological biases, the meta-analysis amplifies them, it does not correct them.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Publication bias**: studies with significant results are more easily published $\Rightarrow$ the available literature is distorted.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Excessive heterogeneity**: studies that are too different in terms of populations, measures, interventions — the pooled estimate can be clinically meaningless ("apples and oranges").

\vspace{0.3em}

**Data duplication**: multiple studies on the same dataset are not independent.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Outcome switching**: primary studies selectively report the significant outcomes.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Aggregation bias (ecological fallacy)**: group-level effects do not necessarily apply to the individual.

## Psychometrics and meta-analysis

There are several approaches aimed at incorporating elements of **psychometrics** (e.g. reliability) into the results of meta-analyses.

This can be done "analytically" or through RoB checklists.

Examples of analytical methods:

-   Hunter & Schmidt (2015) *Methods of Meta-Analysis: Correcting Error and Bias in Research Findings.*

-   Jak & Cheung (2020) \href{https://files.osf.io/v1/resources/ce85j/providers/osfstorage/5bf7eb3ec21cd8001aa31ac9?action=download&version=4&direct}{\underline{\textbf{Download Article}}}

Examples of Checklists

-   Mokking et al. (2024). The COSMIN 2.0 checklist ... *Quality of Life Research.* \href{https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11541334/pdf/11136_2024_Article_3761.pdf}{\underline{\textbf{Download Article}}}

## Systemic Problems in Clinical Evidence

\small

**Small N and underpowered studies**

The majority of studies in neuropsychology have samples that are insufficient to detect small effects. With small samples, if the effect is significant, it tends to be overestimated (**Winner's curse**).

\vspace{0.4em}

**Questionable Research Practices (QRPs)**

\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item \emph{$p$-hacking}: testing multiple analyses until $p < 0.05$
\item \emph{HARKing}: Hypothesizing After Results are Known — presenting as hypothesis what emerged from the data
\item \emph{Outcome switching}: changing the primary outcome after seeing the data
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.4em}

# 12.4 Guidelines and Consensus

## Clinical Guidelines

\small

**Guidelines** translate scientific evidence into recommendations for clinical practice.

\vspace{0.4em}

**How they are built**:

\begin{enumerate}\footnotesize
\item Multidisciplinary panel of experts
\item Systematic review of the available evidence
\item Assessment of the quality of evidence using the \textbf{GRADE} system
\item Formulation of recommendations (strength and direction)
\item External review and periodic updating
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.4em}

## The GRADE system

**GRADE system** (Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation):

\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item Quality of evidence: High / Moderate / Low / Very Low
\item Strength of recommendation: Strong / Weak (conditional)
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2em}

\tiny Guyatt et al. (2008). GRADE: An emerging consensus. \emph{BMJ} 336:924. Open Access: \url{https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.39489.470347.AD}

## Delphi Consensus

\small

When evidence is scarce or insufficient for guidelines based on systematic reviews, the **Delphi method** is used to synthesize **expert opinion** in a structured way.

\vspace{0.4em}

**Procedure**:

\begin{enumerate}\footnotesize
\item Selection of a panel of experts (anonymity guaranteed)
\item Round 1: open questionnaire — experts express their opinions
\item Summary of responses and feedback to the panel
\item Round 2 (and following): experts re-evaluate their positions in light of the group
\item The process is repeated until \textbf{consensus} is reached (e.g.\ $\geq 70\%$ agreement)
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.4em}

## Delphi in neuropsychology or psychology

**Use in neuropsychology**: definition of diagnostic criteria (e.g. MCI), recommendations for neuropsychological assessment in rare conditions, standards of practice in the absence of RCTs.

\vspace{0.2em}

\tiny Fink (2019). \emph{The Delphi Method: Technique and Applications}.

## GRADE and Strength of Recommendations

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners, text width=4.5cm, align=center,
              font=\footnotesize, minimum height=0.9cm},
]
\node[box, fill=mblue!80, text=white] at (0,4)
  {\textbf{High quality}\\\tiny Well-conducted RCTs or highly consistent observational evidence};
\node[box, fill=mblue!55, text=white] at (0,2)
  {\textbf{Moderate quality}\\\tiny RCTs with limitations or prospective observational studies};
\node[box, fill=mblue!30] at (0,0)
  {\textbf{Low quality}\\\tiny Observational studies or RCTs with serious limitations};
\node[box, fill=mblue!12] at (0,-2)
  {\textbf{Very low quality}\\\tiny Expert opinions, case reports, studies with serious risk of bias};
\node[right, font=\scriptsize, mblue] at (2.5,4) {$\Rightarrow$ \textbf{Strong} recommendation};
\node[right, font=\scriptsize, mblue] at (2.5,-2) {$\Rightarrow$ \textbf{Weak} recommendation};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

# Section 2 Summary

## Summary: From a Single Study to Practice

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners, fill=mblue!20, text width=3.2cm,
              align=center, font=\footnotesize, minimum height=0.85cm},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth}, mblue, thick}
]
\node[box] (st) at (0,4.5)  {Primary studies\\(Section 1)};
\node[box] (sr) at (0,3.3)  {Systematic Review\\(+ PRISMA)};
\node[box] (ma) at (0,2.1)  {Meta-analysis\\(Forest + Funnel Plot)};
\node[box] (gr) at (-2.2,0.9){GRADE\\(quality of evidence)};
\node[box] (dp) at ( 2.2,0.9){Delphi Consensus\\(expert opinion)};
\node[box] (lg) at (0,-0.2) {Guidelines\\(clinical practice)};
\draw[arr] (st)--(sr); \draw[arr] (sr)--(ma);
\draw[arr] (ma)--(gr); \draw[arr] (ma)--(dp);
\draw[arr] (gr)--(lg); \draw[arr] (dp)--(lg);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

```{=html}
<!--
## Open Access Resources — Section 2

\small

\begin{itemize}\footnotesize

\item \textbf{PRISMA 2020}: Page et al.\ \emph{BMJ} 372:n71.\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.n71}

\item \textbf{PRISMA-ScR} (Scoping Review): Tricco et al.\ \emph{PLoS Med} 2018.\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pmed.1002544}

\item \textbf{RoB 2} (Risk of Bias RCT): Sterne et al.\ \emph{BMJ} 2019.\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.l4898}

\item \textbf{GRADE}: Guyatt et al.\ \emph{BMJ} 336:924, 2008.\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.39489.470347.AD}

\item \textbf{Publication Bias}: Carter et al.\ \emph{Adv. Methods Pract. Psychol. Sci.} 2019.\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1177/2515245919847196}

\item \textbf{$p$-hacking and QRPs}: Simmons et al.\ \emph{Psychol. Sci.} 2011.\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611417632}

  \item \url{https://mentalhealth.bmj.com/content/ebmental/22/4/153.full.pdf}

\item \textbf{Meta-analysis in R}: \texttt{metafor} package (Wolfgang Viechtbauer).\\
  \url{https://www.metafor-project.org} --- Open Access tutorials available

\item \textbf{Cochrane Handbook} (complete guide to systematic reviews):\\
  \url{https://training.cochrane.org/handbook}

\end{itemize}
-->
```
